---
title: "Pulse Prune"
subtitle: "Cut weights that stay dim. Watch the network rewire around you."
description: "A browser mini-game from MLSys Playground. Magnitude-based pruning on a live, fine-tuning network. Teaches iterative pruning, the lottery-ticket hypothesis, and the coupled dynamics of compression and training."
page-layout: article
format:
  html:
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          <link rel="stylesheet" href="/assets/games/common.css">
          <script src="/assets/games/common.js" defer></script>
          <script src="/assets/games/prune.js" defer></script>
---

```{=html}
<div class="mlsp-game-container" role="region" aria-label="Pulse Prune mini-game">
  <canvas id="mlsp-canvas" class="mlsp-game-canvas" width="680" height="460"></canvas>
  <div class="mlsp-game-hud">
    <span class="mlsp-score"><span id="mlsp-acc">100.0</span>% acc · <span id="mlsp-spa">0</span>% sparse · ×<span id="mlsp-combo">1</span> combo</span>
    <span>click dim weights · <kbd>R</kbd> retry</span>
  </div>
</div>

<div id="mlsp-aha-slot"></div>

<script>
(function mountPulsePrune(){
  function boot() {
    if (!window.MLSP || !MLSP.games || !MLSP.games.prune) {
      return setTimeout(boot, 30);
    }
    var canvas = document.getElementById("mlsp-canvas");
    var $acc   = document.getElementById("mlsp-acc");
    var $spa   = document.getElementById("mlsp-spa");
    var $combo = document.getElementById("mlsp-combo");
    var ahaSlot = document.getElementById("mlsp-aha-slot");
    var api = MLSP.games.prune(canvas, {
      onScoreChange: function(s) {
        $acc.textContent = s.accuracy.toFixed(1);
        $spa.textContent = s.sparsity.toFixed(0);
        $combo.textContent = s.combo;
      },
      onGameOver: function(result) {
        MLSP.showAhaCard(ahaSlot, api.ahaLabel, api.ahaText);
        // Append a share button to the aha card region
        var card = ahaSlot.querySelector(".mlsp-aha-card");
        if (card && api.buildShareText) {
          var share = document.createElement("button");
          share.type = "button";
          share.textContent = "📋 Copy share text";
          share.style.cssText = "margin-top:0.75rem;background:#a31f34;color:#fff;border:none;padding:0.4rem 0.85rem;border-radius:4px;cursor:pointer;font-size:0.85rem;font-weight:600;";
          share.addEventListener("click", function() {
            var txt = api.buildShareText(result);
            if (navigator.clipboard) {
              navigator.clipboard.writeText(txt).then(function(){
                share.textContent = "✓ Copied!";
                setTimeout(function(){ share.textContent = "📋 Copy share text"; }, 1800);
              });
            }
          });
          card.appendChild(share);
        }
      },
      onRetry: function() { window.location.reload(); }
    });
  }
  boot();
})();
</script>
```

## What you're doing

Each tick (twice a second), the network fine-tunes itself on an imaginary stream of training data — weight magnitudes visibly shift, some thicken, some thin. Every second or so, a sample flashes through left-to-right and gets classified; green **✓** if the network got it right, red **✗** if not. If you sit still too long, the accuracy ceiling starts to drift down — "staleness" — and the network slowly forgets.

Your job: **click weights to prune them**. Dim, stable-small weights are redundant — cutting them is nearly free and the combo meter rewards you for finding them. Bold, active weights are load-bearing — cut one and accuracy *plunges*, with screenshake as the tell.

The core trick you develop after a few rounds: **redundancy is dynamic**. A weight that looks safe at 30% sparsity is often load-bearing at 60%, because the surviving weights have to carry the same signal through fewer paths. Good players cut a few, pause, let the network re-stabilise, then cut a few more.

## Why this matters in real ML systems

Deployed neural networks are expensive — every weight costs memory, bandwidth, and energy on every inference. **Pruning** removes weights that contribute least to model output, shrinking models 5×–20× with minimal accuracy loss at scale. But production pruning is never one-shot: it's *iterative* — prune a slice, fine-tune to recover, prune again — because the network genuinely rewires around your cuts. The **lottery-ticket hypothesis** ([Frankle & Carbin, 2018](https://arxiv.org/abs/1803.03635)) claims that dense networks contain sparse sub-networks which, trained from scratch, match full-model accuracy. Finding those winning tickets is an active research area. You just played the most intuitive version of that search.

## Daily puzzle

Every day, everyone playing Pulse Prune starts from the same seeded network. Beat your daily best, then come back tomorrow for a fresh one. Share your score from the aha card above.

---

*Part of [MLSys Playground](/games/) — small games that make ML systems constraints playable.*
